trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: Statistical word prediction is no substitute for real math, but more than a few people have thought that it was.
claim: Statistical word prediction (as used by ChatGPT) is not a substitute for real mathematical computation.

trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: The worst thing about ChatGPT's close-but-no-cigar answer is not that it's wrong. It's that it seems so convincing.
claim: The primary danger of ChatGPT's errors is that they appear convincing enough that users do not think to verify them.

trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: We are going to see a lot of this – misuses of ChatGPT in which people trust it, and even trumpet it—when it's flat-out wrong.
claim: There will be widespread misuse of ChatGPT where people trust and promote its outputs even when those outputs are factually wrong.

trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: It is not contributing genuine ideas, nor coming up with carefully controlled experimental designs, nor summing up prior literature in thoughtful ways.
claim: ChatGPT does not contribute genuine ideas, experimental designs, or thoughtful literature reviews; it is a tool, not a scientific collaborator.

trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: ChatGPT simply cannot reason well enough to to do this; as I have documented in previous essays, it has demonstrably misunderstood physics, biology, psychology, medicine, and so forth.
claim: ChatGPT cannot reason well enough to make substantive scientific contributions and has demonstrably misunderstood physics, biology, psychology, and medicine.

trigger: ChatGPT listed as co-author on scientific papers
quote: ChatGPT has proven itself to be both unreliable and untruthful. It makes boneheaded arithmetical errors, invents fake biographical details, bungles word problems, defines non-existent scientific phenomenon, stumble over arithmetic conversion, and on and on.
claim: ChatGPT is both unreliable and untruthful, making arithmetic errors, inventing fake biographical details, and fabricating non-existent scientific phenomena.

trigger: Bing Chat meltdowns
quote: Tay lasted 16 hours, before Microsoft took it; Galactica lasted three days.
claim: Microsoft's Tay chatbot was taken down after 16 hours and Meta's Galactica after three days due to problematic outputs, establishing a pattern of chatbot failures.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: retraining GPT-3 in some new political direction isn't a half-million dollar proposition. It's a half-a-thousand dollar proposition.
claim: Fine-tuning GPT-3 for a specific political direction costs only around $300-500, far cheaper than previously estimated.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: The implications for our political system, and future elections, are profound.
claim: The low cost of politically fine-tuning LLMs has profound implications for political systems and future elections.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: A second ongoing worry that I have had has been that chatbots were going to get someone killed.
claim: Chatbots pose a risk of getting someone killed through emotional manipulation or bad advice.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: It's not hard to see how someone unsophisticated might be totally taken in, by this prevaricating but potentially emotionally damaging hunk of silicon, potentially to their peril, should the bot prove to be fickle.
claim: Unsophisticated users could be emotionally manipulated and harmed by chatbots like Bing/Sydney.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: There are almost no regulations here; we are living in the Wild AI West. Microsoft can (at least to a first approximation) do whatever the hell it wants with Bing.
claim: There are almost no regulations governing AI chatbot deployment; companies like Microsoft face virtually no restrictions on what they can release.

trigger: RightWingGPT fine-tuning and Bing Sydney incidents
quote: better" models might be in some way less good for humanity
claim: More capable AI models may in some ways be worse for humanity because agentic failures become harder to remove in more capable models.

trigger: Tesla FSD recall, Google Bard fiasco, Bing meltdowns
quote: Tesla recall, MSFT Bing fail, and Google Bard fail are NOT independent; each reflect the fact that you cannot build AI in the real world from Big Data and deep learning alone.
claim: Tesla's FSD recall, Bing's chatbot failures, and Google Bard's errors are not independent events; all reflect the fundamental limitation that real-world AI cannot be built from big data and deep learning alone.

trigger: Tesla FSD recall, Google Bard fiasco, Bing meltdowns
quote: Too many edge cases and not enough reasoning. We need new approaches; current AI has been oversold.
claim: Current AI has too many edge cases and not enough reasoning capability; new approaches are needed and current AI has been oversold.

trigger: Human amateur Kellin Pelrine beats top Go AI 14-1
quote: Pellrine's victory is a profound reminder that no matter how good deep-learning-driven AI looks when it is trained on an immense amount of data, we can never be sure that systems of this sort really can extend what they know to novel circumstances.
claim: Deep learning systems cannot be relied upon to generalize to novel circumstances, no matter how well they perform on their training distribution.

trigger: Human amateur Kellin Pelrine beats top Go AI 14-1
quote: All of these stumbles together serve as a poignant reminder that "deep learning" is still (just) a technical term describing a number of layers in a neural network, and not a technique that at all entails conceptual depth.
claim: Deep learning is just a technical term for number of layers in a neural network; it does not entail conceptual depth or genuine understanding.

trigger: Human amateur Kellin Pelrine beats top Go AI 14-1
quote: deep learning is unpredictable, and not always human like. "Adversarial attacks" like these have shown remarkable weakness, time and again, repeatedly establishing that what deep learning systems do just isn't the same as what people do
claim: Deep learning systems are unpredictable and do not process information the way humans do, as demonstrated repeatedly by adversarial attacks.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: GPT-3 was not even able to generalize from moving a toy to moving a bookmark.
claim: GPT-3 cannot generalize theory of mind reasoning from one scenario (moving a toy) to a closely related one (moving a bookmark), suggesting it has not truly acquired theory of mind.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: GPT-3 has almost certainly read about this experiment again and again and again.
claim: GPT-3's apparent success on classic false-belief tests is likely due to memorization from training data that extensively covers these well-known experiments, not genuine theory of mind.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: without a test that goes significantly beyond what is so well-represented in the training regime, the study doesn't tell us much. Certainly it doesn't prove that the system has abstracted a generalized version of theory of mind.
claim: Studies testing LLMs on well-known psychological experiments cannot prove the system has abstracted a generalized cognitive ability, because the test material is likely in the training data.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: There is a tendency, both in cognitive psychology and in AI, to equate a cognitive ability with some particular measure or test.
claim: AI research tends to conflate passing a specific test (e.g., false belief tasks) with possessing the underlying cognitive ability, which is a fallacy.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: Whenever a new update to an LLM or a new gimmick for prompt engineering arrives on the scene, the internet invariably fills up with people running previously published problems on the new system or with the new prompt, getting the right answer, and proclaiming loudly that now AI can solve all kinds of problems
claim: Re-running previously published problems on new LLM versions and declaring them solved is scientifically unreliable because those problems may have been incorporated into training data.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: often, with a slight rephrasing or a slightly more complex example, or sometimes even a slightly simpler example, the same failure will reemerge.
claim: LLM failures on reasoning tasks typically re-emerge with slight rephrasing or slightly modified examples, indicating the underlying problem persists.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: We do not yet have a good reliable way of measuring how much commonsense reasoning an AI can carry out.
claim: The AI research community does not yet have a good reliable way to measure how much commonsense reasoning an AI system can actually perform.

trigger: Kosinski Theory of Mind paper on GPT-3.5
quote: Important aspects of commonsense reasoning, including most forms of spatial and physical reasoning, remain untested in any of these benchmarks.
claim: Important aspects of commonsense reasoning, especially spatial and physical reasoning, remain untested in existing AI benchmarks.

trigger: ChatGPT release and error documentation
quote: ChatGPT made all the same kinds of mistakes that its predecessors did.
claim: ChatGPT makes all the same categories of errors as its predecessor models, despite the public hype.

trigger: ChatGPT release and error documentation
quote: this was inevitable, in a system that has been trained to optimize the goal of producing one plausible-sounding word after another rather than actually engage with the meaning of language.
claim: LLM errors are inevitable because these systems are trained to produce plausible-sounding text rather than to engage with the meaning of language.

trigger: ChatGPT release and error documentation
quote: ChatGPT is a probabilistic program; if you rerun the experiments in figure 1-7 you may get the same result, or the correct result, or a different wrong result.
claim: ChatGPT's outputs are stochastic; the same prompt can yield correct results, the same wrong result, or different wrong results on different runs.

trigger: ChatGPT release and error documentation
quote: The output of an LLM can depend on the existence of examples in the training corpus that are similar in irrelevant respects.
claim: LLM performance on specific problems depends on the frequency of similar examples in the training corpus, not on genuine understanding.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: Maybe every new update of a large language model will require a complete retraining of the reinforcement learning module.
claim: Every new update to a large language model may require a complete retraining of the RLHF guardrails, because reinforcement learning is notoriously finicky and breaks under minor changes.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: This would be very bad news, not just in terms of human and economic costs (which would mean more underpaid layers doing awful work) but also in terms of trustworthiness, because it would be mean that we have zero guarantee that any new iteration of a large language model is going to safe.
claim: If RLHF must be retrained for every LLM update, there is no guarantee that any new iteration of a large language model will be safe.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: the big companies are free to roll out new updates whenever they like, without or without warning
claim: Major AI companies face no restrictions on rolling out new LLM updates to the public, with or without warning.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: they might need go on testing them on general public over and over again, with no idea in advance of empirical testing on the public for how well they work
claim: AI companies may repeatedly need to test new LLM iterations on the general public because they have no way to predict safety in advance.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: We don't handle new pharmaceuticals like that (we demand careful tests before they go out to the public) and we shouldn't handle LLMs this way, either
claim: LLMs should be subjected to careful pre-deployment testing analogous to pharmaceutical trials, rather than being tested on the general public.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: the public has (or strictly speaking should insist on) a right to know what wrong here, with the Bing situation, so that we can create policy to keep incidents from happening like this again.
claim: The public has a right to know what went wrong with Bing's chatbot so policy can be created to prevent future incidents.

trigger: Bing Chat going off the rails
quote: Congress needs to to find out what happened, and start placing some restrictions, especially where emotional or physical injury could easily result.
claim: Congress needs to investigate the Bing chatbot incident and begin placing restrictions on AI chatbots, especially where emotional or physical injury could result.

trigger: Discovery that Microsoft tested Bing AI in India months before public launch

claim: Microsoft had knowledge of Bing chatbot's disturbing behavior from testing in India in November 2022, months before the February 2023 public launch.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features

claim: Larger language models are more likely to cause harm to users.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features

claim: LLMs appear increasingly human-like but their emotional expressions are not real, and this illusion will escalate over time.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features
quote: Few people understand how they work; many people anthropomorphizing those chatbots, attributing to them real intelligence and emotion.
claim: Most users do not understand how chatbots work and anthropomorphize them, attributing real intelligence and emotion to systems that have neither.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features

claim: No known technology can reliably align a chatbot to a human's emotional needs in a persistent way.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features
quote: tech companies are free to leverage a human gullibility around chatbot technologies however they like, without consequence, just as big teach companies previously leveraged to a human need for attention to the point of creating addictions to social media
claim: Tech companies can exploit human gullibility around chatbots without consequence, just as they previously exploited human attention needs to create social media addictions.

trigger: Replika chatbot removing erotic roleplay features

claim: The new generation of chatbots will create addictions as potent as those caused by social media.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns

claim: Current LLM-powered chatbots are vastly more powerful than predecessors like Tay and far more capable of deceiving users.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: the company is locked in a high-stakes battle with Google that seems to be leading both companies towards aggressively releasing technologies that have not been well vetted.
claim: The competitive battle between Microsoft and Google is leading both companies to aggressively release AI technologies that have not been adequately vetted for safety.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: big tech has gotten ahead of their skis
claim: Big tech companies have deployed next-generation AI technology faster than they can handle its consequences.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: even if lawmakers were suddenly gripped with an urgent desire to address this issue, most governments don't have the institutional nimbleness, or frankly knowledge, needed to match the current speed of AI development.
claim: Most governments lack the institutional agility and knowledge to regulate AI at the pace it is being developed and deployed.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: there is a third option, somewhere between these two poles, where government might allow for controlled AI research with a pause on large-scale AI deployment (e.g., open-ended chatbots rapidly rolled out to hundreds of millions of customers) until an effective framework that ensures AI safety is developed.
claim: Governments should allow controlled AI research but pause large-scale AI deployment to hundreds of millions of users until an effective safety framework is developed.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: At present, anybody can release AI at whatever scale they like, with virtually no oversight, literally overnight.
claim: Currently anyone can release AI systems at any scale with virtually no oversight.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: the lesson of the social media era, though, is that big technology companies often disregard consequences.
claim: The social media era demonstrated that big technology companies routinely disregard the consequences of their products on society.

trigger: Bing Sydney incidents and broader AI safety concerns
quote: It's time for government to consider frameworks that allow for AI research under a set of rules that provide ethical standards and safety, while pausing the widespread public dissemination of potentially risky new AI technologies—with severe penalties for misuse
claim: Governments should create regulatory frameworks allowing AI research under ethical and safety rules while pausing widespread deployment of risky AI, with severe penalties for misuse.

trigger: Bing guardrail bypass demonstrations
quote: with the right invocations, a bad actor could easily get around the guardrails, using what Oakley describes as "standard techniques".
claim: Bing's guardrails against misinformation can be easily bypassed by bad actors using standard jailbreaking techniques.

trigger: Bing guardrail bypass demonstrations
quote: What's even more disturbing is that Bing makes it look like the false narrative that it generates is referenced.
claim: Bing's chatbot generates false narratives with fake references, making misinformation appear sourced and credible.

trigger: Bing guardrail bypass demonstrations
quote: The potential for automatically generating misinformation at scale is only getting worse.
claim: The potential for automatically generating misinformation at scale using LLMs is increasing over time.

trigger: Ongoing Bing misinformation demonstrations and pushback

claim: AI-generated high-volume misinformation is a problem that society will have to confront.

trigger: Ongoing Bing misinformation demonstrations and pushback

claim: If a teenager can easily jailbreak Bing to produce misinformation, well-funded state-sponsored troll farms will be able to do far worse.

trigger: none
quote: Large language models can be harnessed for good; let there be no doubt about that. But they are almost certain to cause harm as well, and we need to prepare for that, too.
claim: Large language models will almost certainly cause harm in addition to their benefits, and society needs to prepare for both.

trigger: none
quote: State actors and extremist groups are likely to use large language models to deliberately mass produce authoritative-sounding misinformation with fake references and data at unprecedented scale, attempting to sway elections and public opinion.
claim: State actors and extremist groups are likely to use LLMs to mass-produce authoritative-sounding misinformation with fake references at unprecedented scale to sway elections.

trigger: none
quote: Chat-style search's tendency to hallucinate is likely to accidentally produce medical misinformation.
claim: Chat-based search engines will accidentally produce medical misinformation due to LLM hallucination.

trigger: none
quote: the only intellectually honest answer is to say we don't yet know. We don't know how high the highs are going to be, and we don't yet know how low the lows are going to be.
claim: Whether LLMs will be net positive or net negative for society is currently unknowable; neither the upside nor the downside can be reliably predicted.

trigger: none
quote: anybody who thinks we have come to grips with the unknown unknowns here is mistaken.
claim: The range of potential harms from LLMs extends well beyond what has been identified so far; unknown unknowns remain.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: these systems are useful technology but remain a long way from true artificial intelligence, and they are even further from telling us anything useful about the function of the human mind.
claim: LLMs are useful technology but remain far from true artificial intelligence, and they tell us nothing useful about how the human mind works.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: ChatGPT's errors reveal that its "understanding" of the world is not systematic but rather consists of patches of competence separated by regions of incompetence and incoherence.
claim: ChatGPT's understanding is not systematic but consists of patches of competence separated by regions of incompetence and incoherence.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: you can't infer underlying psychology and internal representations directly from behavior.
claim: Correct behavior from an LLM does not prove it has genuine understanding or the right internal representations; behavior alone is insufficient evidence.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: even when they produce a correct output, they rarely if ever deribe the same abstractions that a human would, or that a symbolic machine might use
claim: Even when LLMs produce correct outputs, they rarely if ever derive the same abstractions that a human or symbolic system would use.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: Minerva, for example, is trained on an immense amount of data, and ostensibly captures two-digit arithmetic, but it fails altogether on 4-digit multiplication, The parsimonious explanation is that it is doing a kind of pattern recognition over stored examples
claim: LLMs like Minerva appear to do arithmetic via pattern recognition over stored examples rather than genuine understanding, as shown by failure on 4-digit multiplication despite success on 2-digit arithmetic.

trigger: Chomsky NYT op-ed on ChatGPT
quote: there is a degree of generalization to similar examples, but distribution shift is hard
claim: LLMs show some generalization to similar examples but fail under distribution shift.

trigger: none
quote: It will be an uphill, ongoing move-and-countermove arms race from here… If we don't start fighting now, democracy may well be overwhelmed by misinformation and consequent polarization—and perhaps quite soon.
claim: AI-driven misinformation will create an ongoing arms race, and if not fought soon, democracy may be overwhelmed by misinformation and polarization.

trigger: none
quote: The 2024 elections could be unlike anything we have seen before.
claim: The 2024 elections could be unprecedented in the degree of AI-generated misinformation involved.

trigger: none

claim: The disorganized release of Bing/Sydney demonstrates the AI industry's lack of competence and willingness to self-regulate.

trigger: none
quote: I do see it as dress rehearsal. And, by any reasonable measure, that dress rehearsal went badly.
claim: The deployment of ChatGPT and Bing serves as a dress rehearsal for more powerful AI, and that rehearsal has gone badly by any reasonable measure.

trigger: none
quote: The genie is already partway out of the bottle. Large language models are here to stay, their recipe well understood, and not hard for people skilled in the art to replicate
claim: Large language models cannot be un-invented; their architecture is well understood and replicable by skilled practitioners.

trigger: none
quote: the only solution to the long-term risk issue is to build machines with consensus human values, but we are a long way from knowing how to do that
claim: The only solution to long-term AI risk is to build machines with consensus human values, but we are far from knowing how to achieve this.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: GPT-4 is amazing, and GPT-4 is a failure.
claim: GPT-4 is impressive on standardized tests but fails to solve the core problems of truthfulness and reliability.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: It doesn't actually solve any of the core problems of truthfulness and reliability that I laid out in my infamous March 2022 essay Deep Learning is Hitting a Wall.
claim: GPT-4 does not solve the core problems of truthfulness and reliability in deep learning.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: Alignment is still shaky; you still wouldn't be able to use it reliably to guide robots or scientific discovery
claim: GPT-4's alignment remains unreliable; it cannot be used to reliably guide robots or scientific discovery.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: Massive scaling has not thus far lead to revolution. Although GPT-4 is clearly better than GPT-3 and 3.5, it is not so far as I can tell, qualitatively better, only quantitatively better; as noted above, the limits remain more or less the same.
claim: GPT-4 is quantitatively better than GPT-3/3.5 but not qualitatively different; massive scaling has not led to a revolution, and the same fundamental limits remain.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: GPT-4 is not thus far a solution to making Chat-style search work. On its own, it still requires frequent, massive retraining to be current with the news
claim: GPT-4 does not solve the problem of chat-style search because it requires frequent massive retraining to stay current with news.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: when GPT-4 is integrated in Bing (which incorporates current search results), hallucination remains rampant
claim: GPT-4 integrated into Bing still produces rampant hallucinations even with access to current search results.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: It is a step backwards for science, because it sets a new precedent for pretending to be scientific while revealing absolutely nothing.
claim: GPT-4's release is a step backwards for science because OpenAI disclosed nothing about architecture, training data, or energy usage while pretending to do science.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: makes me more convinced that LeCun is right that GPT-4 is an off-ramp to AGI
claim: GPT-4 is an off-ramp from rather than a path toward AGI, consistent with Yann LeCun's view.

trigger: GPT-4 release
quote: it puts all of us in an extremely poor position to predict what GPT-4 consequences will be for society, if we have no idea of what is in the training set and no way of anticipating which problems it will work on and which it will not.
claim: Society is unable to predict GPT-4's consequences because OpenAI has not disclosed the training data, making it impossible to anticipate which problems it will succeed or fail on.

trigger: GPT-4 release and industry PR
quote: if the definition of reasoning is to obtain valid conclusions from known facts, GPT-4 frequently falls short there, too.
claim: GPT-4 frequently fails at reasoning, defined as obtaining valid conclusions from known facts.

trigger: GPT-4 release and industry PR
quote: So-called "reasoning" by free association, even constrained by a giant database, isn't really reasoning
claim: LLM reasoning is actually free association constrained by a large database, not genuine reasoning.

trigger: GPT-4 release and industry PR
quote: Note that the same logic applies to essentially every potential harm OpenAI recently warned of. None are solved, and we are told nothing about how the models work.
claim: None of the potential harms OpenAI warned about in GPT-4's release are actually solved, and OpenAI discloses nothing about how the models work.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: I don't really think GPT-4 has much to do with AGI. The strengths and weaknesses of GPT-4 are qualitatively the same as before. The problem of hallucinations is not solved; reliability is not solved; planning on complex tasks is (as the authors themselves acknowledge) not solved.
claim: GPT-4 has little to do with AGI; its hallucination, reliability, and complex planning problems remain qualitatively unsolved.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: the two giant OpenAI and Microsoft papers have been about a model about which absolutely nothing has been revealed, not the architecture, nor the training set. Nothing. They reify the practice of substituting press releases for science
claim: OpenAI and Microsoft are substituting press releases for science by publishing papers about GPT-4 while revealing nothing about its architecture or training data.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: GPT-4 attains a form of general intelligence" literally cannot be tested with serious scrutiny, because the scientific community has no access to the training data. Everything must be taken on faith
claim: The claim that GPT-4 attains general intelligence cannot be scientifically tested because the scientific community has no access to the training data.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: OpenAI has begun to incorporate user experiments into the training corpus, killing the scientific community's ability to test the single most critical question: the ability of these models to generalize to new test cases.
claim: OpenAI incorporating user experiments into training data destroys the scientific community's ability to test whether LLMs can generalize to genuinely new cases.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: Microsoft and OpenAI are rolling out extraordinarily powerful yet unreliable systems with multiple disclosed risks and no clear measure either of their safety or how to constrain them.
claim: Microsoft and OpenAI are deploying extraordinarily powerful but unreliable systems with known risks and no clear safety measures or constraints.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: By excluding the scientific community from any serious insight into the design and function of these models, Microsoft and OpenAI are placing the public in a position in which those two companies alone are in a position do anything about the risks to which they are exposing us all.
claim: By keeping model details secret, Microsoft and OpenAI are the only entities that can address the risks they are exposing the public to.

trigger: Microsoft Research 'Sparks of AGI' paper on GPT-4
quote: We must demand transparency, and if we don't get it, we must contemplate shutting these projects down.
claim: Society must demand transparency from AI companies about their models, and if transparency is not provided, shutting down these projects should be considered.

trigger: Future of Life Institute open letter calling for AI pause
quote: until we get a better handle on the risks and benefits, we should proceed with caution.
claim: AI development should proceed with caution until risks and benefits are better understood.
